title: User Interaction Mastery
tagline: Forms, events, and real user experiences.

accomplishments: Build a signup form that checks if inputs are valid
Create real-time validation that gives instant feedback
Stop bad data before it gets saved

do.title: Build a Simple Form Validator
steps: Try submitting with empty fields — see error messages!
do.code:
Sign Up Form

✅ Form submitted successfully!
do.result: JavaScript VALIDATED the inputs before accepting them. This prevents bad data!
do.proofRequired: Screenshot showing error messages for both fields when validation fails.

concepts.name: Getting input values with .value
concepts.definition: document.getElementById('field').value returns whatever the user typed. It's always a string, even for number inputs.
concepts.analogy: Reading what someone wrote on a form — .value is the answer they filled in.

concepts.name: Checking if empty
concepts.definition: if (value === "") checks if the user left the field blank. Empty string means nothing was typed.
concepts.analogy: Checking if a box is empty before shipping it.

concepts.name: Checking string length
concepts.definition: .length works on strings just like arrays. "hi".length is 2. Use it to enforce minimum/maximum character counts.
concepts.analogy: Measuring a piece of string — is it long enough for what you need?

concepts.name: .includes() for content checks
concepts.definition: string.includes('@') returns true if the string contains that character. Great for email validation.
concepts.analogy: Searching a document for a specific word — is it in there or not?

concepts.name: classList.add() and .remove()
concepts.definition: Add or remove CSS classes with JavaScript. This lets you change styling (red borders, green borders) without editing CSS.
concepts.analogy: Putting on or taking off a costume — the person (element) is the same, but they look different.

concepts.name: oninput for real-time validation
concepts.definition: The oninput event fires every time the user types a character. Use it for live feedback like password strength checkers.
concepts.analogy: A spell-checker that underlines words as you type, not after you finish.

understand.keyTakeaway: Good validation: (1) Get the value, (2) Check if it's valid, (3) Give helpful feedback. Use .value, .length, .includes(), and classList.

build.goal: Build a password strength checker with real-time feedback
build.hint: oninput fires on EVERY keystroke. That's why it updates instantly as you type!

challenge.title: Build a Complete Registration Form
challenge.mission: Create a full registration form that validates username, email, password, confirm password, and age.
requirements: Show specific error messages for each failure
Green borders for valid, red for invalid
challenge.starterCode: <!-- Build a complete registration form with 5 validated fields -->

capabilities: Add/remove CSS classes for visual feedback

bridge.preview: In the next lesson, you'll learn how to handle EVENTS — clicks, keypresses, mouse movements, and more. You'll build interactive games and tools.

accomplishments: Detect keyboard presses and respond to them
Build a game you control with arrow keys
Create interactive experiences that feel alive

do.title: Build a Keyboard-Controlled Box
steps: Click anywhere on the page first (to give it focus)
Press the arrow keys — watch the red box move!
do.code:
🎮 Keyboard Controller

Use Arrow Keys to move the red box
X: 375, Y: 225
do.result: JavaScript listened for keyboard events and moved the box based on which arrow key you pressed!
do.proofRequired: Screenshot showing the box moved to a different position.

concepts.name: What is an event?
concepts.definition: Something that happens in the browser — a user action (click, keypress, mouse move) or browser action (page loaded). JavaScript can listen for these and respond.
concepts.analogy: A motion sensor — it detects when something happens and triggers a response.

concepts.name: addEventListener() vs onclick
concepts.definition: addEventListener('click', function) is the modern way. Better than onclick because: separates HTML/JS, allows multiple listeners, gives you the event object, can be removed.
concepts.analogy: onclick is a sticky note on one button. addEventListener is a proper notification system.

concepts.name: The event object
concepts.definition: The function you pass to addEventListener receives an event object with info about what happened: event.key (which key), event.type (what kind), event.target (which element).
concepts.analogy: A detective report — it tells you what happened, where, when, and how.

concepts.name: Keyboard events: keydown and keyup
concepts.definition: keydown fires when a key is pressed. keyup fires when released. event.key gives the key name: 'ArrowUp', 'Enter', 'a', ' ' (space).
concepts.analogy: keydown = pressing a piano key. keyup = releasing it.

concepts.name: Browser coordinate system
concepts.definition: X increases left to right. Y increases top to bottom (opposite of math class!). (0,0) is the top-left corner.
concepts.analogy: Reading a page — you start at the top-left and go right and down.

concepts.name: setInterval() for timed events
concepts.definition: setInterval(function, milliseconds) runs code repeatedly at a set interval. clearInterval(timer) stops it. 1000ms = 1 second.
concepts.analogy: An alarm clock that goes off every X seconds until you turn it off.

concepts.name: Creating elements dynamically
concepts.definition: document.createElement('div') creates a new element. Set its properties, then element.appendChild(newElement) adds it to the page.
concepts.analogy: Building a Lego piece (create), decorating it (set properties), then snapping it onto your model (appendChild).

understand.keyTakeaway: addEventListener() is the modern way to handle events. The event object tells you everything about what happened.

build.goal: Build a Click Speed Game with targets, score, and countdown timer

codeComparison.old:
// Old way: onclick in HTML
<button onclick="doSomething()">Click</button>

build.hint: setInterval() keeps running until you stop it with clearInterval(). That's how the countdown works!

challenge.title: Build a Keyboard Piano
challenge.mission: Create a virtual piano where pressing A, S, D, F, G, H, J lights up corresponding piano keys.
requirements: Each responds to a keyboard letter (A-J)
Keys light up on keydown, return to normal on keyup
Display which note is being played
Support both keyboard and mouse click
challenge.starterCode:
<!-- Build a piano with .key elements using data-key attributes -->
<!-- Listen for keydown to add "active" class -->
<!-- Listen for keyup to remove "active" class -->
<!-- Use document.querySelector(`.key[data-key="${key}"]`) to find matching key -->
challenge.bonusChallenge: Add actual sound using the Web Audio API or HTML5 <audio> elements!

capabilities: Use addEventListener() to handle events properly
Detect keyboard presses with keydown and keyup
Access event info through the event object
Use data attributes to connect HTML and JavaScript

bridge.preview: Fantastic! You can now handle all kinds of user interactions. In the next lesson, you'll learn complex conditionals, switch statements, and smart decision-making in your code.

accomplishments: Build a quiz app that gives different responses based on answers
Create a shipping calculator with complex pricing rules
Make a recommendation engine that combines multiple conditions

do.title: Build a Multi-Condition Checker
steps: Try different combinations — age 15 with ticket, age 10 with/without parent
do.code: 🎬 Movie Theater Entry
do.result: JavaScript checked MULTIPLE conditions together using AND (&&) and OR (||) operators!
do.proofRequired: Screenshot showing the "allowed" result when age is 13+ with a ticket.

concepts.name: AND operator (&&)
concepts.definition: Both conditions must be TRUE for the whole thing to be TRUE. if (age >= 18 && hasTicket) only runs if BOTH are true.
concepts.analogy: You need BOTH a key AND the correct password to open the safe.

concepts.name: OR operator (||)
concepts.definition: At least ONE condition must be TRUE. if (age >= 65 || age <= 12) runs if either is true.
concepts.analogy: You can enter if you have a ticket OR you're a VIP member — one or the other works.

concepts.name: NOT operator (!)
concepts.definition: Flips TRUE to FALSE and FALSE to TRUE. if (!hasTicket) runs when hasTicket is false.
concepts.analogy: The opposite. If door is NOT locked, you can enter.

concepts.name: Switch statements
concepts.definition: Cleaner alternative to long if/else chains when checking one variable against multiple specific values. Don't forget the break statement!
concepts.analogy: A train track switch — route the train (code) to the correct track (case) based on the destination (value).

concepts.name: Truthy and falsy values
concepts.definition: Falsy: false, 0, "", null, undefined, NaN. Everything else is truthy. if (userName) runs if userName has any text.
concepts.analogy: A yes/no decision — anything "empty" or "zero" counts as no, everything else counts as yes.

concepts.name: Comparison vs Assignment
concepts.definition: = assigns a value (x = 5). === checks equality (x === 5). Using = in an if statement is a common bug that makes the condition always true.
concepts.analogy: = is putting water IN a glass. === is asking "is there water in the glass?"

understand.keyTakeaway: && means "both must be true", || means "at least one must be true", ! means "flip it". Switch is cleaner than long if/else chains.

build.goal: Build a food ordering system using a switch statement
build.hint: break exits the switch statement. Without it, execution "falls through" into the next case, which is usually a bug!

challenge.title: Build a Shipping Calculator
challenge.mission: Create a shipping cost calculator with complex rules based on weight, destination, and order value.
requirements: Free shipping if order ≥ $100 AND not express
challenge.starterCode:
<!-- Build a shipping calculator using switch + logic operators -->
<!-- Use parseFloat() to get number values from inputs -->
<!-- Use switch statement for destination multiplier -->
<!-- Use && (AND) to check for free shipping condition -->
challenge.bonusChallenge: Add a "Fragile Item" checkbox that adds 20% to the shipping cost!

capabilities: Combine conditions with AND (&&) and OR (||)
Use NOT operator (!) to flip conditions
Use switch statements for multiple options

bridge.preview: In the next lesson — the Module 3 CAPSTONE — you'll combine EVERYTHING from this module (form input, events, keyboard controls, and smart logic) to build a complete, playable Space Shooter game!

lessons.title: Module 3 Capstone: Build a Space Shooter Game

accomplishments: Build a complete, playable Space Shooter game
Combine keyboard input, timers, and collision detection
Track score, lives, and game state like a real game developer
Create start and game over screens with restart
Save high scores that persist using localStorage
Ship something you can actually play and share!

do.title: Build a Complete Space Shooter
steps: Copy the complete game code below
Use Arrow keys to move, Space to shoot
Destroy enemies to score points — survive as long as you can!
do.code:
SCORE: 0 LIVES: 3 HIGH: 0

🚀 SPACE SHOOTER

Arrow Keys to move, Space to shoot

GAME OVER

Final Score: 0
do.result: You built a COMPLETE, playable Space Shooter game! Keyboard controls, collision detection, score tracking, lives, game over, and persistent high scores — all in one file.
do.proofRequired: Screenshot showing the game running with at least 50 points scored AND the Game Over screen after losing all lives.

concepts.name: The game loop
concepts.definition: A function that runs 60 times per second with setInterval(loop, 1000/60). It updates positions, checks collisions, and redraws — over and over.
concepts.analogy: A movie projector showing 60 frames per second. Each frame is a tiny update, but they blur together into smooth motion.

concepts.name: Game state object
concepts.definition: One object holds ALL the game's data: score, lives, player position, bullets, enemies. This gives you one source of truth for everything.
concepts.analogy: A game's scoreboard and playing field combined — one place to see the whole status.

concepts.name: Tracking keys with a keys object
concepts.definition: Instead of moving inside the keydown handler, store which keys are down in an object (keys[e.key] = true) and check them in the game loop. This gives smooth continuous movement.
concepts.analogy: keydown alone is like tapping a key once. The keys object is like HOLDING it down — the ship glides smoothly.

concepts.name: Collision detection
concepts.definition: Check if two objects overlap by comparing their positions. Math.abs(a.x - b.x) < threshold && Math.abs(a.y - b.y) < threshold is a quick distance check.
concepts.analogy: Checking if two stickers are close enough on a page that they touch.

concepts.name: Spawning objects dynamically
concepts.definition: Use setInterval to create new enemies at random positions. document.createElement + appendChild adds them to the DOM, and you push them into an array to track them.
concepts.analogy: A factory conveyor belt — new enemies roll out at a fixed rate, each one brand new.

concepts.name: Cleaning up (splice + remove)
concepts.definition: When an enemy is destroyed or goes off-screen, remove it from the DOM (element.remove()) AND from the tracking array (array.splice). If you forget, the game gets slower and slower.
concepts.analogy: Taking out the trash. Leave it and it piles up — eventually the whole game chokes.

concepts.name: Persisting data with localStorage
concepts.definition: localStorage.setItem('key', value) saves data in the browser. It survives page reloads. Use parseInt/JSON.parse to read it back. Perfect for high scores.
concepts.analogy: A save file on your computer. Close the browser, come back tomorrow — your high score is still there.

understand.keyTakeaway: Games are just: (1) a state object, (2) a game loop that updates it, (3) event listeners for input, (4) collision checks, and (5) cleanup. Master this pattern and you can build any 2D game.

build.goal: Understand why we track keys in an object instead of handling them directly
build.hint: The keys object also lets you handle MULTIPLE keys at once — like moving diagonally or moving while shooting. keydown alone can't do that cleanly.

challenge.title: Build Your Own Game (Choose One)
challenge.mission: Pick ONE of these game ideas and build it from scratch using the game loop pattern you learned. This is your chance to prove you've mastered Module 3.
requirements: Option A: Typing Speed Game — words fall from the top, type them before they reach the bottom. Track WPM and accuracy.
Option B: Brick Breaker — paddle at bottom (arrow keys), ball bounces, destroy bricks for points.
Option C: Snake — classic snake that grows when it eats food. Game over when it hits itself.
Option D: Dodger — avoid falling obstacles using arrow keys. Score increases with survival time.
Must include: game state object, game loop with setInterval, keyboard controls, score tracking, game over screen, restart button
Must save a high score using localStorage
challenge.starterCode:
<!-- Start with the game loop skeleton: -->
<!-- 1. Create a state object for your game -->
<!-- 2. Set up a game loop: setInterval(loop, 1000/60) -->
<!-- 3. Track keys with keydown/keyup + keys object -->
<!-- 4. In the loop: update positions, check collisions, cleanup -->
<!-- 5. Update the UI when score/lives change -->
<!-- 6. Save high score to localStorage on game over -->
challenge.bonusChallenge: Add progressive difficulty (enemies spawn faster as score climbs), sound effects with HTML5 <audio>, and a pause feature (P key)!

capabilities: Build a complete, playable 2D game from scratch
Run a 60 FPS game loop with setInterval
Manage game state in a single object
Handle smooth keyboard input with a keys tracker
Dynamically spawn and destroy DOM elements
Implement score, lives, and game over logic
Persist high scores across sessions with localStorage
Clean up resources to keep performance smooth

bridge.preview:
🎉 CONGRATULATIONS! You completed Module 3: User Interaction Mastery! You can now handle forms, events, keyboard input, timers, and complex logic — and build real, interactive games.

Next up: Module 4 — Advanced Array Methods. You'll learn map, filter, and reduce — the trio that transforms how you work with data forever.
